story:
Late in the afternoon in Alpbach on July 2, Paweł and Mary left the dog in the guesthouse and walked down the valley toward the village. The path ran among meadows, past lonely houses and farmsteads, until they reached the tiny old church with a green turret. Paweł then led Mary to a path at the bottom of the village, to a spring flowing from a dragon-shaped spout, a place he remembered from his Alpbach Forum days. Before they reached it, they came upon a wooden chicken coop. A dozen young hens rustled out, perked up, and ran straight at them, flapping. One hen leapt to Paweł’s shoulder and bit his ear. He cried out at the sharp pain, and they both stumbled away until the hens calmed down. They reached the spring, drank its cold water, and, after wandering among houses by the stream called Alpbach, took the mail bus back from Reith im Alpbachtal and returned to their guesthouses.

Mary did not dwell on the hens. The village enchanted her: wooden and brick houses with riotous flowers on their balconies; a church in the center; elegant hotels and villas; soft, meadowed slopes above, dotted with brown cows, goats, and sheep with bells; paved paths rising past cottages, hay lofts, and barns to gentle peaks. It was simply a fairy-tale place. In the east stood the conference center where seminars for the Alpbach Forum were held.

On July 3, at Camila’s suggestion, Paweł and Mary went with Paweł’s spaniel, Dude, to Reith im Alpbachtal and the pretty Reither See. They planned to bathe and let the dog swim. A woman rested on a small beach while her ten-year-old son floated on an inflatable mattress. Mary could swim; Paweł could not, and he sat in the shallows. After a while, a swan glided in from the far side. It swam calmly until the boy drifted near. Then the swan flared its wings, surged forward, and, in agitation, pecked at the mattress until it punctured. The boy, startled, kicked out in panic and began to sink. Mary swam hard, slid under the collapsing mattress, and pulled him to shore. The swan veered away. The mother ran to them, breathless with thanks. Paweł praised Mary’s courage. Mary, still trembling, said, “Birds! They’re attacking again!” She spoke of Bodega Bay and the famous story from 1963, the one about birds turning on people. Paweł tried to calm her. He called these episodes unfortunate incidents. Mary shook her head.

Back in Alpbach that afternoon, after dinner at the “In corner” restaurant, they walked the path starting behind their guesthouses up toward the Trat hill. Sheds and hay stores stood above the forest edge. Mary spread a blanket in the meadow; Paweł sat beside her; Dude sniffed around. Overhead a large raptor circled, a sharp beak and yellow talons catching the light. Suddenly it stooped. It rushed over Mary’s head; she covered her face and bent to the grass as Dude barked. The bird wheeled back and struck Paweł’s back with its beak, then tried to seize the dog, who wriggled free. They ran for an open hay shed and tumbled inside. Blood stained Paweł’s shirt. Mary tore strips to make a dressing. When Paweł cracked the door, the bird stood outside, waiting. Mary trembled and cried. They stayed there until night fell and a full moon lifted. At last the bird was gone. “We must warn everyone,” Mary said. “They can kill.” “I see it now,” Paweł answered. “It wounded me.” Mary wanted to leave Alpbach at once. They agreed to return to the guesthouses first, and then make plans to go.
